text: Hi there, Jim Johnson for Accent Help here. And what I want to do is I want to clarify
something about breath support. I've encouraged you to think about breath support equals abs,
breath support, abs. And getting more specific than that, it's the transversus abdominis,
which is involved in breath support. So most of the time when you're doing a crunch,
when you're doing sort of a traditional sit-up crunch where you're lifting your shoulder blades
off of the floor, what you're actually working is your rectus abdominis. And those are your
ab muscles that go up and down. Then you also have your internal and external obliques,
which move at these angles, which is what you're working when you do some sort of a twisting thing.
The hard one to get at is the transversus, which is down lower underneath and runs across your
body in this direction, sort of like a belt across your body. Now, in order to shift,
you're going to have to do a lot of work. And that's what we're going to do. And that's
going to shift a regular crunch from being about purely the rectus abdominis to being about the
transversus abdominis or the transverse or the transversus or whatever, right? So the belt one
there that we want to get at, the core, what you do is instead of doing a crunch regularly,
you just do a little pelvic tuck. So it's as though I'm going to flatten out my back a little
bit into the floor. If I exaggerate it, it's a big old pelvic thrust kind of thing, right? But I
don't need to go that far. It really can be a little bit more of a stretch. And then you're
going to be almost not visible for you. I just did it. And now I went out of it, going into it
now, right? So it's a tiny movement. And if you make that shift instead of the crunch that I
originally did, doing that crunch now is much harder because it's moving a lot of the work
to the transversus, which isn't used to doing this nearly as much.
You can do a similar thing with your legs in the air, where you maybe cross,
the ankles and you take this and you can do some crunches here, which will primarily work
the rectus abdominis. But if you just do a little pop, think of lifting your butt slightly off the
floor or bringing your knees slightly more towards your head, that will tend to engage
the transversus going across and make, oh, whatever you're doing, focus more of the work
on the transversus. And then it's not just the rectus abdominis working.
So that transversus is the muscle that we want to get going for breath support. We want to be aware
of that little belt, low, engaging for breath support for when we are speaking, for especially
feeling a punch maybe when you say an operative or an important word. This word is very important.
Kick on that. There you go. An intro to the transversus abdominis. Breath support, abs,
transversus.
There you go. For more info, check out AccentHelp.com. Thanks.
